content.text:
*"Teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart 
of wisdom."*{Psalm 90:12}{Beware of bugs in the above code; I have only proved it correct, not tried it.}{Donald Knuth}

How do we measure the efficiency of an algorithm? We use **asymptotic 
notation** to describe how running time or 
space usage grows with input size.

content.title: Big-O
content.content:
Let $f$ and $g$ be functions from $\N$ to $\R^+$. We say $f(n) = O(g(n))$ 
(read "$f$ is big-O of $g$") if there exist constants $C > 0$ and $n_0$ 
such that:
\[ f(n) \leq C \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0 \]

content.text:
Big-O provides an **upper bound** on growth. If $f(n) = O(g(n))$, 
then $f$ grows no faster than $g$ (up to a constant factor) for large $n$.

content.title: Big-O examples
content.problem:
1. $3n + 5 = O(n)$. Choose $C = 4$, $n_0 = 5$: for $n \geq 5$, 
 $3n + 5 \leq 4n$.

2. $n^2 + 100n = O(n^2)$. For large $n$, the $n^2$ term dominates.

3. $\log_2 n = O(\log n)$. All logarithms differ by a constant 
 factor, so we omit the base.

content.title: Big-Omega and Big-Theta
content.content:
$f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ ("big-Omega") if there exist $C > 0$ and $n_0$ 
such that $f(n) \geq C \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$. This provides a 
**lower bound**.

$f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ ("big-Theta") if $f(n) = O(g(n))$ and 
$f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$. This provides a **tight bound**.

content.title: Growth Rate Ordering
content.content:
For large $n$:
\[ 1 < \log n < \sqrt{n} < n < n \log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n! < n^n \]
content.label: Theorem

items: Count the basic operations (comparisons, assignments, etc.).
Express the count as a function of input size $n$.

content.title: Analyzing nested loops
content.problem:
verbatim
 for i from 1 to n do
 for j from 1 to n do
 print(i, j)
 end for
 end for
verbatim

The inner loop runs $n$ times for each of $n$ iterations of the outer 
loop. Total: $n \times n = n^2$ print operations. Time complexity: $O(n^2)$.

content.title: Dependent loops
content.problem:
verbatim
 for i from 1 to n do
 for j from 1 to i do
 print(i, j)
 end for
 end for
verbatim

Total operations: $1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = O(n^2)$.

content.title: Best, Worst, and Average Case
content.content:
An algorithm may perform differently on different inputs of the same size.

- **Best case**: minimum time (often unrealistic)

- **Worst case**: maximum time (guaranteed upper bound)

- **Average case**: expected time over all inputs
When we give a single complexity, we usually mean worst case.

marginNotes.text:
The race is not to the swift or the 
battle to the strong... but time and chance happen to them all.
